content:
4 min read
Just now

--

After years of development, every Angular application accumulates dead code.
Components that nobody renders.
Services nobody injects.
Routes nobody visits.
Assets nobody uses.
The problem is… they’re surprisingly hard to find.
📖 The Story
A few months ago I was working on migrating a large enterprise Angular application.
The project had evolved over several years.

• 1,000+ Angular components

• Hundreds of services

• More than 100 lazy-loaded modules

• Thousands of assets

• Dozens of developers contributing over the years
During the migration, I kept asking the same question:
“Can we safely delete this file?”
Nobody knew.
Some components hadn’t been touched for years.
Some services were replaced long ago.
Entire feature folders were abandoned but still existed in the repository.
Everything still compiled.
Everything still passed CI.
Everything looked “used.”
And that was the problem.
🤔 Why Finding Dead Code in Angular Is Hard
Most developers start with this:
grep -r "UserComponent"Find All References
Seems reasonable…
Unfortunately, Angular isn’t a plain TypeScript application.
A component can be:

• imported

• declared

• exported

• bundled
…and still never be rendered anywhere.
Registration ≠ Usage.
For example:
@NgModule({
declarations: [
OldDashboardComponent
]
})
export class DashboardModule {}grep says:
✅ Used
Reality:
Nobody ever renders
<app-old-dashboard>The component is effectively dead.
😵 Why Existing Tools Fail
Most existing tools understand TypeScript.
Angular is much more than TypeScript.
Angular adds:

• HTML Templates

• Component Selectors

• Pipes

• Dependency Injection

• Lazy Routes

• Standalone Components

• NgModules

• Angular Metadata
Simple text search cannot understand those relationships.
That’s why dead code detection in Angular is much harder than in React.
